---
suppress-bibliography: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

Type 2 diabetes represents a large and increasing global health burden, making prevention a central public health priority [@saeedi2019]. Identifying and characterizing individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes is therefore essential, yet current risk stratification largely relies on glycemic states such as impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance, which reflect a combination of insulin resistance and declining beta-cell function [@defronzo2004]. Although glucose patterns during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) help characterize glycemic states, they provide limited insight into broader metabolic heterogeneity. While heterogeneity in type 2 diabetes is well-established [@ahlqvist2018; @tobias2023], emerging evidence suggests that metabolic heterogeneity is already present before diabetes onset, highlighting an opportunity for more targeted prevention strategies [@wagner2026; @wagner2021].

Beyond glucose, the OGTT captures postprandial hormonal responses that may represent another dimension of metabolic heterogeneity among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes. The incretin effect, which is the elevated insulin response to oral versus intravenous glucose, is primarily driven by glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP), which are important regulators of postprandial insulin response and glucose regulation [@holst2019]. GLP-1 enhances insulin secretion and suppresses glucagon, while GIP primarily stimulates insulin secretion. This makes GLP-1 a key regulator of postprandial glucose, demonstrated by the success of GLP-1-based therapies in treating type 2 diabetes and obesity [@holst2024]. In contrast to the glucose-lowering mechanisms of GLP-1 and GIP, glucagon increases glucose levels by stimulating glycogenolysis in the liver, making its suppression during glucose intake essential for normal glucose regulation [@ahrén2015]. However, the interplay between incretin secretion and glucagon suppression among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism is complex, underscored by conflicting findings in the literature. Incretin secretion has been found to be decreased [@toft-nielsen2001], normal [@vollmer2008] or even increased [@færch2008] among individuals with impaired glucose metabolism. Similarly, glucagon suppression appears to vary with insulin sensitivity, as individuals with prediabetes or screen-detected type 2 diabetes exhibit impaired early but relatively normal late glucagon suppression, whereas individuals with higher insulin sensitivity show higher early but lower late glucagon suppression [@færch2016; @wagner2017].

In addition to postprandial glycemic and hormonal responses, body fat distribution represents another important dimension of metabolic heterogeneity. In particular, visceral adipose tissue (VAT) and subcutaneous adipose tissue (SAT) are closely linked to both insulin resistance and beta-cell function [@kahn2001a], and differences in fat distribution are already observed across normal glucose tolerance, prediabetes and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes [@borel2015]. This is further supported by recent phenotyping studies identifying subgroups with distinct characteristics, including diverse patterns of body fat distribution [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025]. However, while both hormonal responses and body fat distribution are recognized as important contributors to metabolic risk, these dimensions are typically studied separately. Consequently, it remains unclear how OGTT-derived glucose, insulin, incretin and glucagon responses relate to patterns of body fat distribution among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes. The aim of this study was therefore to identify metabolic subgroups in this population using K-means clustering of OGTT-derived glucose and hormonal responses, and to quantify the associations between these subgroups and measures of body fat distribution.
